coredata.dc:title: One-pot facile synthesis of bright blue emitting silicon nanoparticles for sensitive detection of luteolin via inner filter effect
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: PAN, Cong-Jie
CHEN, Tian-Tian
MA, Long-Fei
QIN, Xue-Zhen
WEN, Qiao-Qiao
FENG, Su-Xiang
coredata.dc:description: Luteolin has gained extensive attention in pharmaceutical and clinical fields owing to their beneficial effects for human health. Accurate detection of luteolin is quite important for drug quality control and clinical medicine research. In this work, novel water dispersible fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal approach by using (3-aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane (APTMS) as silicon source and D-penicillamine as reducing agent. The synthesized SiNPs emitted bright blue fluorescence with a high quantum yield of 44.78% and displayed outstanding optical properties and excellent stability. The fluorescence of the SiNPs could be distinctly quenched by luteolin through inner filter effect (IFE). Based on this, a novel fluorescent method for luteolin detection was developed by using the SiNPs as probe. A linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and the concentration of luteolin was obtained from 1 to 100 µM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.24 µM. The proposed method was successfully applied to detect luteolin in urine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon nanoparticles
Fluorescence
Luteolin
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of luteolin in urine samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthetic strategy of the SiNPs; (b) Schematic diagram of the SiNPs for luteolin detection.
(A) TEM image; (B) Corresponding size distribution histogram; (C) PXRD spectra and (D) FT-IR spectra of the SiNPs.

High resolution XPS spectra of the SiNPs: (a) full range, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s, (e) Si 2p, respectively; (f) UV-Vis absorption spectra (a), fluorescence excitation spectra (b), and emission spectra (c) of the SiNPs.

(A) Normalized FL intensity of the SiNPs at different temperature; (B) Normalized FL intensity of the SiNPs at different pH value; (C) Normalized FL intensity of the SiNPs in different concentrations of NaCl solution; (D) The FL intensity variation tendency of the SiNPs as a function of time under 362 nm light illumination. The error bar is the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

(A) Fluorescence responses of the SiNPs upon addition of different concentrations of luteolin in a pH 7.4 PBS solution. (B) log(F 0/F) of the SiNPs as a function of the concentration of luteolin. The error bar is the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

(A) FL intensity of the SiNPs to 300 μM possible interfering ions and the subsequent addition of 30 μM luteolin. (B) FL intensity of the SiNPs to 300 μM possible interfering organic substances and the subsequent addition of 30 μM luteolin. All experiments were carried out in a buffer solution of pH 7.4. The error bar is the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

(A) UV-Vis absorption spectra of luteolin (a), FL excitation spectra (b) and emission spectra (c) of the SiNPs; (B) Suppressed efficiency (E, %) of observed (black line, E obsd) and corrected (red line, E cor) FL intensity.
Detection of luteolin in urine samples.

head.ce:title: One-pot facile synthesis of bright blue emitting silicon nanoparticles for sensitive detection of luteolin via inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Henan University of Chinese Medicine, Zhengzhou 450046, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Pharmacy, Henan University of Chinese Medicine, Zhengzhou, 450046, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Pharmacy, Henan University of Chinese Medicine, Zhengzhou, 450046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy, Henan University of Chinese Medicine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luteolin has gained extensive attention in pharmaceutical and clinical fields owing to their beneficial effects for human health. Accurate detection of luteolin is quite important for drug quality control and clinical medicine research. In this work, novel water dispersible fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal approach by using (3-aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane (APTMS) as silicon source and D-penicillamine as reducing agent. The synthesized SiNPs emitted bright blue fluorescence with a high quantum yield of 44.78% and displayed outstanding optical properties and excellent stability. The fluorescence of the SiNPs could be distinctly quenched by luteolin through inner filter effect (IFE). Based on this, a novel fluorescent method for luteolin detection was developed by using the SiNPs as probe. A linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and the concentration of luteolin was obtained from 1 to 100 µM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.24 µM. The proposed method was successfully applied to detect luteolin in urine samples.

Water dispersible SiNPs with a high quantum yield of 44.78% were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. A novel fluorescent method based on the prepared SiNPs was constructed for sensitive detection of luteolin via inner filter effect. The proposed method was simple in design, rapid and sensitive in response.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Luteolin (3′,4′,5,7-tetrahydroxyflavone), an important bioactive flavonoid, is widely found in many vegetables and fruits. Owing to its unique properties including cardiovascular protection, anti-allergic properties, anti-inflammatory effects, and anticancer activity, luteolin has been widely concerned in pharmaceutical and clinical fields [1,2]. Furthermore, recent studies have shown that luteolin can enter the cell nucleus and inhibit oxidative damage to deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA). Whereas, overuse of luteolin has a pro-oxidant effect, which instead promotes the oxidative damages to DNA [3]. Therefore, developing a facile and sensitive method for luteolin detection is of great significance.

Nowadays, many analytical methods have been reported for the detection of luteolin, including high-performance liquid chromatography [4–6], gas chromatography [7], capillary electrophoresis [8–9] and electrochemical methods [10–12]. Nevertheless, the shortcomings such as expensive and sophisticated instruments, complicated operations and time-consuming hampered their further application to some extent. Compared with the above methods, fluorescent method with outstanding advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity, simple operation, low cost and portability [13–18], is a good choice for luteolin detection. For example, Sun et al. developed a carbon dot based fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of luteolin [19]. Up to now, few fluorescence methods for luteolin detection have been reported. It is still necessary to develop novel environmentally friendly fluorescent probes with excellent properties for sensitive detection of luteolin.

Recently, silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs), a novel kind of environmentally friendly fluorescent nanomaterials, have attracted extensive attention because of their outstanding properties such as excellent photostability, non-toxicity, good water solubility, good biocompatibility and biodegradability [20–23]. Hence, SiNPs are expected to be burgeoning alternatives to toxic quantum dots and commercial fluorescent dyes. The development of novel methods to prepare SiNPs with excellent properties has been a research focus in recent years. At present, the preparation methods of water dispersible SiNPs mainly include post modification method [24], ultraviolet radiation method [25], microwave method [26], and hydrothermal method [23]. Thereinto, hydrothermal method has been widely used due to the virtues of cheap equipment, simple operation, mild reaction conditions and high quantum yield of fabricated materials. Because of their unique properties, many SiNPs based fluorescent probes have been designed for fluorescence detection of target analytes. For instance, Na et al. prepared novel fluorescent silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) by one-step strategy for sensitive and selective detection of K4[Fe(CN)6] [27]. Nsanzamahoro et al. developed a sensitive fluorescent method based on water soluble SiNPs for catechol detection [28]. Han et al. synthesized the chiral SiNPs via one-step aqueous solution synthesis strategy for selective recognition of aminopropanol enantiomer [29]. The above studies suggested that SiNPs have great potential in fluorescence sensing. To our knowledge, fluorescent SiNPs have not been utilized for luteolin detection yet. Inspired by this, a novel fluorescent method based on SiNPs was developed for luteolin detection.

In this work, by using (3-aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane (APTMS) as silicon source and D-penicillamine as reducing agent, novel water dispersible fluorescent SiNPs were synthesized via a facile one-pot hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 a). The resulting SiNPs possessed a bright blue fluorescence with a high quantum yield of about 44.78% and exhibited excellent optical properties, good thermostability, strong photostability and high salt-tolerance. The fluorescence of the SiNPs could be effectively quenched by luteolin. Based on this phenomenon, a novel fluorescent method for the analysis of luteolin was established (Scheme 1b). This method was simple in design, rapid and sensitive in response. Moreover, the proposed method was successfully applied to detect luteolin in urine samples with satisfactory recovery results.

(3-aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane (APTMS), D-penicillamine, luteolin, L-cysteine, L-tryptophan, L-threonine, L-tyrosine, maltose, levodopa, urea, and glucose were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co. Ltd. Calcium chloride (CaCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), potassium iodide (KI), potassium fluoride (KF), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium perchlorate (NaClO4), potassium oxalate monohydrate (K2C2O4·H2O) and sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3) were obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. NaH2PO4·2H2O and Na2HPO4·12H2O were received from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory. Phosphate buffered saline solution (PBS) was prepared with 10 mM NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4. All reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) experiment was performed by a Tecnai G2F30 instrument. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray powder diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were conducted on a Perkin Elmer Frontiera spectrometer using KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha photoelectron spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded by an F-7000 spectrofluorophotometer, and the excitation and emission slits were both set at 5 nm. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra were determined by an EVOLUTION 260 BIO UV-Vis spectrophotometer. Both fluorescence and absorptionmeasurements were carried out in 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cuvettes.

The SiNPs were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method. The detailed experimental procedures were as follows: Firstly, 1 mL APTMS was added into 4 mL deionized water at room temperature. After the solution was stirred evenly, 20.0 mg D-penicillamine was added to the above solution with continuous stirring for about 10 min. After D-penicillamine was completely dissolved, the mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 4 h. After naturally cooled down to room temperature, the obtained SiNPs solution were dialyzed for about 6 h by a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da. Then, the purified SiNPs solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The quantum yield of the SiNPs was calculated by using quinine sulfate as a reference with Eq. (1) [30]:
(1)
φ =
φ 1
A 1
I 1
I A
n 2
n
1 2

In Eq. (1), φ and φ1 are the quantum yield of the SiNPs and quinine sulfate, respectively; A 1 and A are the absorbance of quinine sulfate and SiNPs, respectively; I 1 and I are the integrated emission intensity of quinine sulfate and SiNPs, respectively; n 1 and n are the refractive index of quinine sulfate and SiNPs, respectively.

First of all, the luteolin stock solution was prepared with a concentration of 10 mM by using methanol as solvent. Then, 200 μL SiNPs solution was added into 5.0 mL centrifuge tube followed by different volume luteolin solution and the above solutions were diluted to 2.0 mL with PBS solution (pH 7.4, 10 mM), respectively. The obtained mixture solution was incubated for 1 min at room temperature. The fluorescence emission signals of the above mixture solutions were measured when the excitation wavelength was fixed at 362 nm. The working curve for luteolin detection was plotted by the log (F 0 /F) value of SiNPs at 438 nm and the concentration of luteolin (F 0 and F were the fluorescence intensity (FL intensity) of SiNPs in the absence and presence of luteolin, respectively). To perform the selectivity experiment toward luteolin, various ions (Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, K+, I−, C2O4 2−, Cl−, ClO4 −, F−, and S2O3 2−) and organic molecules (L-tryptophan, L-cysteine, L-tyrosine, L-threonine, maltose, levodopa, urea, and glucose) were tested instead of luteolin under the same conditions as the above detection procedure. Moreover, the anti-interference experiment was also performed by adding luteolin and interfering substances simultaneously under the same conditions.

In this work, APTMS and D-penicillamine were used as silicon source and reducing agent, respectively. The specific synthesis conditions such as the amount of reducing agent, reaction temperature and reaction time were optimized when the volume of APTMS was fixed at 1.00 mL. As illustrated in Fig. S1A, the FL intensity gradually enhanced with the increase of D-penicillamine until to 20 mg, and then slightly decreased with the further increase of D-penicillamine dosage. The result in Fig. S1B showed the obvious increase of the FL intensity by raising the temperature up to 200 °C. As seen in Fig. S1C, the FL intensity gradually increased for 4 h and then stayed nearly stable. Therefore, when the amount of D-penicillamine was 20 mg, the reaction temperature was 200 °C, and the reaction time was 4 h, the FL intensity of the prepared SiNPs was highest. In the control experiment, individual APTMS, D-penicillamine, and the mixture of APTMS and D-penicillamine were reacted under the same conditions, respectively. As seen in Fig. S2, APTMS or D-penicillamine alone could only generate very weak fluorescence. The outstanding FL intensity occurred only when APTMS and D-penicillamine were coexisted.

As shown in Fig. 1 A, the as-prepared SiNPs displayed good dispersity and uniform particle size. The particle size was mainly distributed in the range of 2.0 to 3.6 nm and the mean diameter was about 2.8 nm (Fig. 1B). As see in Fig. 1C, the SiNPs were amorphous state [31]. Fig. 1D was the FT-IR spectra of the SiNPs. As seen in Fig. 1D, the peak at 3417 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of N–H bond [32]. The signals at 2928 and 2876 cm−1 demonstrated the C–H stretching vibration [33]. The band at 1584 cm−1 was assigned to C=O bond [34]. The peaks at 1130 cm−1 and 1025 cm−1 were assigned to the Si–C asymmetric bending and Si–O–Si stretching vibration [35], respectively. The signals of N-H wagging vibration and secondary amine were observed at 763 cm−1 and 676 cm−1, respectively [36]. Then, the structures and functional groups of the SiNPs were further characterized by XPS. Fig. 2 a presented five major peaks at 102.1, 153.1, 285.1, 399.1 and 532.1eV, which were assigned to Si 2p, Si 2s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. Six peaks could be seen in the C 1s spectra (Fig. 2b): C–S (283.1 eV), C–Si (284.5 eV), C–C/C=C (284.6 eV), C–N (285.0 eV), C–OH/C–O–C (285.4 eV), and C=O (288.1 eV) [22]. The N 1s spectra (Fig. 2c) revealed five peaks at 398.8, 398.9, 399.4, 399.6, and 401.8eV, which were attributed to N–S, N–Si, C–N–C, N–H and N–(C)3 bond [37], respectively. The O 1s spectra in Fig. 2d were composed of five peaks at 530.9, 531.7, 532.1, 532.5 and 532.8 eV, which were corresponded to S–O, C–O, Si–O, C–OH/C–O–C and C=O groups [38], respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 2e, four peaks at 102.3, 102.6, 102.8 and 102.9 eV were assigned to Si–C, Si–N, Si–S and Si–O groups [39], respectively. The XPS results were in consistent with that of FT-IR. Fig. 2f was the UV-Vis, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the SiNPs. The SiNPs possessed two absorption peaks at about 216 and 340 nm (curve I), which were originated from the π-π* transition of the C=C band and n-π* transition of the C=O band [40], respectively. As shown in Fig. 2f, the SiNPs solution was transparent, and emitted bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. Moreover, when the excitation wavelength was 362 nm (curve II), the fluorescence emission peak of the SiNPs centered at 438 nm (curve III). Fig. S3 was the fluorescence emission spectra of the SiNPs under various excitation wavelengths (300–390 nm). As seen in Fig. S3, with the constant change of excitation wavelength, the position of the fluorescence emission peak was basically unchanged, indicating an excitation-independent emission behavior [41]. Moreover, the quantum yield of the SiNPs was also measured. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the quantum yield of the SiNPs was about 44.78% (Fig. S4 and Table S1).

The stability of the SiNPs was explored via detecting their FL intensities under different temperatures, pH, high ionic strength medium, and strong light exposure conditions. As seen in Fig. 3 A, the SiNPs showed relatively stable FL intensities as the temperature raised from 25 to 85 °C, demonstrating their good thermal stability. It is illustrated in Fig. 3B that the FL intensity of the SiNPs was stable in the range of pH 4.0 to 10.0, indicating their high pH stability. Moreover, it could be seen in Fig. 3C that the SiNPs exhibited high stability even under 100 mM NaCl solution, showing the good salt tolerance. The stability of the SiNPs under long continuous irradiation was further studied. As seen in Fig. 3D, the SiNPs exhibited outstanding photobleaching resistance after continuous irradiation at 362 nm for 60 min. The above results suggested that the SiNPs possessed excellent stability.

The sensitivity of luteolin detection was improved by optimizing the detecting conditions of the pH of the buffer solution and the incubation time. As seen in Fig. S5A, sensitive and stable responses were obtained in the pH range of 7.0 to 8.0. Thus, pH 7.4 was selected as the buffer solution pH value. The FL intensities were also recorded in different reaction time of 0 to 12 min. It is illustrated in Fig. S5B that the fluorescence quenching rapidly reached stable within 1 min. Therefore, the incubation time was fixed at 1 min.

The sensitivity of the SiNPs for luteolin detection was studied by adding different concentrations of luteolin into the SiNPs solution. As seen in Fig. 4 A, as the luteolin concentration continuously increased from 0 to 100 µM, the FL intensities of the SiNPs decreased gradually. Between the quenching efficiencies log(F 0 /F) and luteolin concentration, a good linear correlation (R 2 = 0.9974) was obtained in the range of 1 to 100 µM (Fig. 4B). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.24 µM, which was calculated by 3s/k (s was the standard deviation of the blank solution and k was the slope of the calibration curve). The sensitivity of this method is comparable with previous works. Compared to published methods (Table S2) [6,9,12,19], this method possessed the advantages of nontoxic and environmentally friendly, one step detection, simple operation, wide linear range, and excellent selectivity for luteolin detection.

Selectivity of the SiNPs towards luteolin detection was conducted in the following experiment. Different ions (Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, K+, I−, C2O4 2−, Cl−, ClO4 −, F−, and S2O3 2−) and some organic substances (L-tryptophan, L-cysteine, L-tyrosine, L-threonine, maltose, levodopa, urea, and glucose) were selected as potential interferences. As seen in Fig. 5 , only the addition of luteolin could result in a distinct fluorescence quenching, indicating the good selectivity. The anti-interference experiment was also conducted by mixing luteolin and interfering substances into the same sensing system. Fig. 5 illustrated that the interfering substances above have little influence on the fluorescence detection of luteolin. Such results demonstrated that this method revealed excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability.

The sensing mechanism of the SiNPs to luteolin was discussed in the following experiment. As shown in Fig. 6 A,the experimental UV-Vis absorption spectra of luteolin were overlapped with the excitation spectra of the SiNPs to a large extent, suggesting that luteolin induced fluorescence quenching of the SiNPs was considered to derive from inner filter effect (IFE) [42]. As known, the lifetime of a fluorescent material is unaffected by IFE. Therefore, in order to verify the existence of IFE, the fluorescence lifetimes of the SiNPs in the absence and presence of luteolin were determined. The results indicated that the fluorescence lifetimes of the SiNPs were basically unchanged when different concentrations of luteolin were added into SiNPs solution (seen in Fig. S6 and Table S3), which indicated that the sensing mechanism of the proposed method was IFE. Then, the contribution of IFE to the quenching process was further investigated by Eq. 2 [43]:
(2)
F cor
F obsd
=
2.3 d
A
e x
1 −
10
− d
A
e x
10
g
A
e m
2.3 s
A
e m
1 −
10
− s
A
e m

In eq. 2, F obsd and F cor are the observed maximum FL intensity and corrected maximum FL intensity after removing IFE from F obsd, respectively; A ex and A em are the absorbance at the maximum excitation wavelength (λ = 362 nm) and maximum emission wavelength (λ = 438 nm), respectively. Additionally, g is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of cuvette (g = 0.25 cm); s is the thickness of the excitation beam (s = 0.50 cm); d is the width of the cuvette (d = 1.00 cm) (illustrated in Fig. S7). The correction factor (CF) values of IFE were calculated according to Eq. 2. According to the reported literature, the maximum CF values could not be greater than 3 [44]; otherwise the correction is not reliable. Table S4 displayed the CF values and some relevant parameters. Among them, E obsd and E cor are the observed and corrected fluorescence quenching efficiencies after adding different concentrations of luteolin into the SiNPs, respectively. Fig. 6B showed that approximately 92.78% of the whole quenching efficiency resulted from IFE. Therefore, IFE was the main mechanism for the fluorescent quenching of the SiNPs toward luteolin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of luteolin in urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the proposed method, the fluorescent response of the SiNPs to luteolin was tested in urine samples. The recoveries were evaluated by adding different known concentrations of luteolin. As illustrated in Table 1 , the average recoveries of luteolin in urine samples were in the range of 99.53%–103.31%. These results indicated that the established method possessed great potential in detecting luteolin in real samples.

In summary, environmental friendly blue emitting SiNPs with a high quantum yield of 44.78% were prepared via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method without further modification. The SiNPs possessed good water solubility, outstanding thermostability, salt tolerance and photobleaching resistance. It was found that the fluorescence of the SiNPs could be effectively quenched by luteolin owing to the IFE mechanism, which allowed it desirable for SiNPs to be used for luteolin detection, with a linear range of 1–100 μM and LOD of 0.24 μM. Furthermore, the proposed method could be applied to detect luteolin in urine samples with satisfactory recoveries.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from the Zhongjing Scholars Research Funding of Henan University of Chinese Medicine (No. 00104311-2021-1-12).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2022.100069.
